subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Rational Function Analysis & Improper Integrals
stimulus: Consider the family of functions $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{x^2 - 2x + k}$, where $k$ is a constant.

parts.prompt: Find the value of $k$, for $k > 0$, such that the slope of the line tangent to the graph of $f$ at $x = 0$ equals 6.

scoring_points.description: Correct computation of $f'(x)$ using the quotient rule: $f'(x) = \dfrac{(x^2 - 2x + k) - x(2x - 2)}{(x^2 - 2x + k)^2} = \dfrac{-x^2 + k}{(x^2 - 2x + k)^2}$
Correct evaluation at $x = 0$: $f'(0) = \dfrac{k}{k^2} = \dfrac{1}{k}$, then setting equal to 6

parts.prompt: For $k = -8$, find the value of $\displaystyle\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx$.

scoring_points.description: Correct substitution $k = -8$: $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{x^2 - 2x - 8} = \dfrac{x}{(x-4)(x+2)}$, then use partial fractions: $\dfrac{x}{(x-4)(x+2)} = \dfrac{A}{x-4} + \dfrac{B}{x+2}$, giving $A = \dfrac{2}{3}$, $B = \dfrac{1}{3}$

parts.prompt: For $k = 1$, find the value of $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} f(x)\,dx$ or show that it diverges.

scoring_points.description: Correct substitution $k = 1$: $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{x^2 - 2x + 1} = \dfrac{x}{(x-1)^2}$; identifies that there is a vertical asymptote at $x = 1$ on $[0, \infty)$, making this an improper integral requiring separate limits: $\int_0^1 \dfrac{x}{(x-1)^2}\,dx + \int_1^{\infty} \dfrac{x}{(x-1)^2}\,dx$

required_elements: Recognizes $f(x) = \dfrac{x}{(x-1)^2}$ has a vertical asymptote at $x = 1$; sets up limit(s)

scoring_points.description: Correct antidifferentiation using partial fractions: $\dfrac{x}{(x-1)^2} = \dfrac{1}{x-1} + \dfrac{1}{(x-1)^2}$, so $\int \dfrac{x}{(x-1)^2}\,dx = \ln|x-1| - \dfrac{1}{x-1} + C$

Correct conclusion: the integral diverges, because $\lim_{x \to 1^-} \left(\ln|x-1| - \dfrac{1}{x-1}\right) = -\infty$ (the $-1/(x-1)$ term $\to +\infty$ but $\ln|x-1| \to -\infty$, and the combined limit diverges)

required_elements: The integral diverges; limit as $x \to 1^-$ or $x \to 1^+$ does not exist as a finite value
